title: Simple 2D Background Art in LibreSprite and Using What We've Made in Godot
content: Simple 2D Background Art in LibreSprite and Using What We’ve Made in Godot This tutorial walks through a beginner-friendly workflow: Create simple 2D background asset and platform in LibreSprite Export them as PNG files Import and place them in a Godot 2D scene Build a simple 2D scene Import and place a 3D asset in a 2D scene Prerequisites LibreSprite installed Godot 4.x installed The 3D Orange from last tutorial Optional: graphics tablet, but mouse works fine. Plan Your Background Assets Before drawing, decide what layers your scene needs. A simple example: Background Sky (solid color or gradient) Mid tree Foreground Platform Recommended asset sizes Pick a target game resolution first (example: 1280x720). Your asset resolution will follow from that decision. Your sky background will want to be the full size of a screen. A mid ground tree might be 256px or 512px tall. Example folder plan: assets/backgrounds/sky_back.png assets/backgrounds/trees_mid.png assets/backgrounds/foreground_plat.png Create Assets in LibreSprite Create a new canvas In LibreSprite: File -> New Set width/height (example 1280 x 720) Background: transparent (for non-sky layers) For pixel art, use integer scaling and keep brush sizes small (1–4 px). Set up palette and style Keep color count small and readable: 1–2 sky colors 2–3 tree/ground colors This keeps backgrounds cohesive and fast to make. Draw by layer depth (far to near) Create separate LibreSprite files (or layers) for: sky mid near Export PNG files For each layer: File -> Export Format: PNG Keep transparency for non-sky layers Use clear names, e.g.: sky_back.png trees_mid.png foreground_plat.png Import 2D Assets into Godot Copy files into project folder Place exported PNGs into your Godot project, for example: res://assets/ Godot auto-imports assets when files appear in the project. Build the Background for a Scene in Godot Create a 2D background scene by creating a sprite for each of the 2D assets we made: Example hierarchy: Sprite2D(sky_back.png) Sprite2D(foreground_plat.png) Sprite2D(trees_mid.png) Adding the 3D Orange Create the 3D render viewport Create new scene Click + (Add Child Node). Add SubViewport. Rename it to OrangeViewport. With OrangeViewport selected, in Inspector: Set Size = 512, 512 (or your preferred size) Set Transparent Bg = On Ensure Disable 3D = Off (must allow 3D) Build the 3D mini-scene inside the viewport Select OrangeViewport → Add Child Node → Node3D. Rename to OrangeWorld. Select OrangeWorld → add Camera3D. Select OrangeWorld → add DirectionalLight3D. Add your orange model: Select the .glb/.gltf of the Orange from last tutorial: drag it from FileSystem onto OrangeWorld. Position camera and light Select Camera3D: Set Position to something like (0, 0, 3). Enable Current = On. - Select DirectionalLight3D: Rotate it so the model is lit (example X -45, Y 30, Z 0). Save 3D Scene/ CTRL + S to save 3D render port. Rename it to 3D_Orange.tscn. Display viewport output in 2D Scene Go to previous 2D scene where we set up out background sprites Select Background sprite → Add Child Node → Sprite2D. Rename to OrangeSprite. Add the orangeViewport scene as a child of OrangeSprite by dragging 3D_Orange.tscn onto OrangeSprite Resize for the Scene Select OrangeSprite, in Inspector: Texture → click dropdown → New ViewportTexture Click the new ViewportTexture resource to edit it Set Viewport Path (or Viewport Path in Scene) to OrangeViewport - Move OrangeSprite to where you want it in the 2D scene. Run and test Press F6 (run current scene). Confirm 3D orange appears in 2D Scene.

title: Simple 3D Orange Tutorial
content: Simple 3D Orange Tutorial Modelling Delete the default cube: Select cube > X > Delete. Add a UV Sphere: Shift-A > Mesh > UV Sphere. Leave segments/rings default. Shade Smooth: With sphere selected in Object Mode, Right-click > Shade Smooth. Enter Edit Mode: Tab. Enable Proportional Editing: press O (toggle on). Select the top vertex: use Vertex select (1 or the toolbar), click the top vertex. Move it down slightly to create a small recess: press G then Z, drag down while mouse wheel adjusts influence radius; make a shallow recess. Add a Subdivision Surface modifier: Properties > Modifiers tab (wrench icon) > Add Modifier > Subdivision Surface. Keep Levels View and Render at the defaults. Add a simple stem: Shift-A > Mesh > Cylinder. In the Add dialog (bottom-left) Move the cylinder into the recess (G), then scale in Edit Mode: select top face > S to scale down. Position and parent: position stem so it sits in the recess; optionally Shade Smooth the stem and join to the orange visually (Object > Set Origin > Origin to Geometry if needed). Texturing Open the Shading workspace (top tabs) and select the sphere object. Click New to create a material. Base color: set Principled BSDF Base Color to hex #ED8800 (a good orange color). Node additions and wiring (Shift-A to add nodes): Add Voronoi Texture Add 2 ColorRamp Add Displacement Connect: Voronoi Texture Distance to both -> Color Ramp Factor Connect: one Color Ramp Color -> “Roughness” Color Ramp` Color -> “Height” Connect: Displacement Height -> Material Output Displacement Play around with the parmeters. The screenshot below has the precise parameters used in the .blend attached to this tutorial: Stem material: Click on the cylinder (while in object mode) and then create a second material for the cylinder. Use a green base (e.g., #3F9300FF). Similar process as with the sphere. See the node setup below: Quick export File > Save As to save your .blend. Render > Render Image (F12). When finished: Image > Save As to export PNG.
